exemplar.title: Will the abundance of Littorina obtusata (Flat periwinkles) change depending on the abundance of Fucus vesiculosus (Bladder wrack)?

detailedExemplarData.title: Will the abundance of Littorina obtusata (Flat periwinkles) change depending on the abundance of Fucus vesiculosus (Bladder wrack)?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is embedded in a clearly defined ecological and geographical context (Jetty Beach, Ballantine exposure scale 6, mid-shore fucoid zone).
Methodological considerations (quadrat sampling, percentage cover, Spearmanâs rank) are well justified in terms of relevance and sufficiency of data.
weaknesses: The methodology description omits details on how percentage cover was standardized (e.g. gridded overlay or photo analysis).
Sampling intervals and quadrat-flip orientation rules are not specified, leaving minor ambiguities for reproducibility.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a specific and appropriate context and fully explains methodological choices, placing two strands at band 5. However, omissions in the methodology details (e.g. standardization of percentage cover, grid coordinates) introduce ambiguities that drop the methodology description into band 3â4. On balance, a band 4 (4 marks) reflects strong performance with some reproducibility gaps.

strengths: Raw data and processed ranks are presented in tables, allowing a reader to follow the calculation of Rs.
The write-up acknowledges qualitative errors in percentage cover estimation, showing awareness of data limitations.
weaknesses: There is no quantitative treatment of uncertainties (no error bars, confidence intervals or Â± estimates).
Several ranking and DÂ² calculations are inconsistent or inaccurate, and large tables split across pages reduce precision and readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording is clear but not precise (band 3), consideration of uncertainties is minimal (band 1), and data processing contains significant errors (band 1â2). These combine to a band 1â2 performance, warranting 2 marks overall.

strengths: The conclusion directly references the calculated Rs value and critical threshold, consistently linking back to the analysis.
Relevant literature on periwinkleâfucoid interactions is cited to justify the ecological interpretation of results.
weaknesses: The biological effect size and potential management implications are not discussed, limiting the broader impact of the conclusion.
Depth of comparison to scientific context is solid but could be expanded with additional studies or meta-analysis evidence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is relevant and consistent with the statistical analysis (band 4) and makes a justified comparison to accepted scientific context (band 5). Minor superficiality in ecological interpretation limits this to the lower end of 5â6, so a band 3â4 places it at 4 marks overall.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (subjective percentage estimation, snail misidentification) are identified and their likely impacts on data validity are explained.
Realistic improvements (use of gridded quadrat, second observer verification) directly address identified limitations.
weaknesses: Quantitative estimation of the magnitude of misidentification or estimation error is not provided, limiting assessment of relative impact.
Some minor methodological issues (e.g. pH recording) are mentioned but not fully explored in the evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific weaknesses and proposes relevant improvements, placing it in band 3â4. However, without quantification of impact or coverage of all minor issues, performance aligns with the lower half of this band, meriting 3 marks overall.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion rejects the null hypothesis and directly references the calculated Rs and critical value, maintaining consistency with the analysis.  Minor doubts about processing accuracy prevent a fully justified (5-6) award, but relevance and general consistency are achieved.

Raw data and processed ranks are presented in tables, but formatting is cluttered with extraneous symbols and the large ranking table is split across pages, reducing precision.  Nevertheless, a reader can follow the values and locate the calculated Rs, so communication is clear OR precise but not both.

The student carries out ranking and computes an Rs value, but several ranks and DÂ² values are mathematically inconsistent (e.g. difference of 20, negative 12), indicating inaccuracies.  Processing is relevant yet contains significant errors, so it fits the descriptor for 1-2 marks with the higher mark in that band.

Two concrete weaknesses (subjective percentage estimation and missed/false snail identification) are explained alongside their likely impact on data accuracy and validity.  The discussion links each weakness to potential skewing of correlation strength, demonstrating an explanation of relative impact.

The student cites two external studies that corroborate the observed relationship and explains mechanistic reasons (food, shelter, camouflage) that align with accepted marineâecology theory.  These comparisons are explicitly used to justify the conclusion, fulfilling the âjustified through relevant comparisonâ descriptor; minor superficiality in depth keeps the score to 5 rather than 6.

The student clearly embeds the research question within a well-defined ecological and geographical setting (Jetty beach, Ballantine exposure scale 6, mid-shore zone, specific fucoid species).  Relevant biological background for both organisms is supplied, establishing why the relationship is worth investigating.  Because the context is not merely general coastal ecology but the precise site and tidal height actually sampled, the description satisfies the descriptor of a âspecific and appropriate context.â

Apart from a qualitative acknowledgement that estimating percentage cover "may not be entirely accurate", no quantitative uncertainties (e.g. Â± estimates, error ranges, confidence in counts) are provided for raw or processed data.  This is only limited evidence of uncertainty treatment.

Improvements (use of gridded quadrat, additional observer time, second observer for verification) are realistic and directly address each stated weakness, with explanations of how they would enhance accuracy and validity.  The reasoning meets the requirement for âexplainedâ improvements, though not all minor issues are covered, so a 5 is appropriate.

The report explains why systematic sampling, a quadrat of fixed size, percentage cover estimates, and Spearmanâs rank were chosen, linking each method to how sufficient and relevant data would be generated for a correlation study.  It also justifies control of tidal height, time and exposure level, and discusses environmental and safety constraints.  These explanations go beyond simple listing and therefore reach the âexplainedâ level.

Step-by-step instructions (locate 3 m above chart datum, place quadrat, estimate cover, count snails, flip quadrat 30 times) would allow another investigator to reproduce the work with only minor ambiguities (e.g. no detail on how percentage cover was estimated or how quadrat flips handled obstacles).  These small omissions place the description in the âfew ambiguitiesâ rather than fully unambiguous category.

annotations.comment: The research question is situated within a precise ecological and geographical context (mid-shore Jetty Beach, Ballantine exposure 6, fucoid zone), providing clear justification for the investigationâs scope and relevance.

The description of initial observations shifts from holdfast counts to percentage cover without detailing how cover was estimated consistently. Clarify the method (e.g.
use of gridded overlay) to support reproducibility.
The background section on Jetty Beach clearly defines the wave exposure context (Ballantine scale 6) and explains its ecological implications, effectively linking site selection to study aims.
Including GPS coordinates and map scale in Figures 1 and 2 would strengthen spatial precision and aid reproducibility for future investigators wishing to replicate site conditions.
The species account of Littorina obtusata provides relevant taxonomic, morphological, and ecological information, establishing a solid biological rationale for the periwinkleâwrack relationship.
Separate headings for Hâ and Hâ repeat shared wording. Consider consolidating into a single section that clearly contrasts null and alternative predictions to improve flow.

The controlled variables section lists many constants but assumes environmental parameters (e.g.
temperature, pH) without measurement. Recording these variables would validate the assumption of constancy.
The sampling methodology omits the interval or step length between quadrat placements and the orientation rule for flips. Specify the sampling grid and flipping pattern to ensure reproducibility.
Safety considerations are thorough, addressing sun exposure, slip hazards, and tidal risks, demonstrating proactive planning for fieldwork health and safety.
The methodology step for percentage cover estimation lacks detail on how to standardize visual scoring. Incorporating a gridded quadrat or photoâanalysis protocol would improve precision.
Listing a waterproof datasheet or digital data logger in equipment would reduce transcription errors and support accurate data recording under field conditions.

The findings report mean values but omit measures of spread (e.g.
standard deviation or range). Including uncertainty estimates would strengthen the data analysis.
The discussion acknowledges qualitative cover estimation error, demonstrating awareness of limitations in data quality.

Table 1 contains rank differences that appear inconsistent (e.g.
DÂ²=400 corresponds to |R1âR2|=20). Review rank assignment and DÂ² calculations for accuracy.
Splitting large tables across pages reduces precision. Consolidate the ranking table or provide a clear continuation header to enhance readability.
The statistical section could discuss Spearmanâs test assumptions (monotonicity, independence) to demonstrate a full understanding of the chosen analysis.
The conclusion correctly compares the calculated Rs value to the critical threshold (0.795 > 0.467) and justifies rejection of Hâ.
Extend the conclusion by discussing ecological effect size or potential management implications to connect statistical results with biological significance.
The conclusion is well supported by comparison to relevant literature on fucoidâperiwinkle interactions, reinforcing the studyâs validity.
The evaluation explains the subjective nature of percentage estimation and its likely impact on data accuracy, demonstrating a clear identification of methodological weakness.
Quantify the frequency or magnitude of misidentified periwinkles in evaluation to illustrate the relative impact of this limitation on the correlation strength.
The suggestion to use a gridded quadrat and a second observer is realistic and directly addresses the identified weaknesses, enhancing data precision and validity.
The controlled variables section is well justified but overly assumes constancy of parameters like pH. Recording these would further validate environmental control.
The alternative hypothesis heading closely mirrors the null hypothesis section. Merging them under one header with clear labels Hâ and Hâ could streamline the reportâs structure.

No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The research question is stated without context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting data relevant to the research question are stated.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data lacks the detail to allow for the investigation to be reproduced.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced with few ambiguities or omissions.
The research question is outlined within a broad context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are described.
The research question is described within a specific and appropriate context.
Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are explained.
The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.
The recording and processing of the data is communicated but is neither clear nor precise.
Some processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with major omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The recording and processing of data shows limited evidence of the consideration of uncertainties.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with some significant omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is either clear or precise.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of a consideration of uncertainties but with some significant omissions or inaccuracies.
The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.
The recording and processing of data shows evidence of an appropriate consideration of uncertainties.
The communication of the recording and processing of the data is both clear and precise.

rubricCriterionStrands.name: Communication of data recording and processing
